title: What I learned shipping AI agents to production
excerpt: Agents demo beautifully and ship painfully. Notes on tool-calling, guardrails, and the boring infrastructure that actually decides whether they survive contact with real users.

body.text: A working agent demo and a production agent are almost two different products. The demo needs one happy path to look magical. Production needs every unhappy path to fail safely — and there are a lot of them.
Tool-calling is the easy 80%

Wiring an LLM to functions is mostly solved. The hard part is everything around it: validating arguments, handling partial failures, retrying idempotently, and making the model recover gracefully when a tool returns something it did not expect.
The model is the cheapest, least reliable component in the system. Design around that, not against it.
Guardrails are a product surface
Refusals, rate limits, and scope boundaries are not afterthoughts — users feel them constantly. Treat them like UI: clear, fast, and honest about what the agent can and cannot do.
The agents that made it to production were never the most capable ones. They were the most predictable.

title: Building a frontend that survives the backend
excerpt: Most frontend bugs are really data bugs wearing a costume. How I structure React apps so the UI degrades gracefully when the server has a bad day.

body.text: Every interface is a bet about the shape of your data. The interesting question is what happens when that bet is wrong — when the field is null, the array is empty, or the request just never comes back.
Model the loading states first
Before I write the happy-path render, I enumerate loading, empty, error, and partial states. If those four feel right, the success state almost designs itself.
A UI that only looks good with perfect data is a prototype, not a product.
The .NET side and the React side are one system. The cleanest frontends I have built came from treating the API contract as a shared design artifact, not a handoff.

title: Thinking like an attacker makes you a better builder
excerpt: Security is not a feature you add at the end. It is a way of reading your own systems — assuming every input is hostile and every boundary is a target.

body.text: The most useful security habit has nothing to do with tools. It is a question you ask constantly: if I wanted to break this, where would I start?
Every input is hostile
Once you internalize that, validation stops feeling like busywork and starts feeling like the actual product. The same instinct makes you write clearer, more defensive code everywhere else.
Offense and defense are the same skill pointed in opposite directions.
Learning to attack systems made me far more careful about the ones I build. The two practices feed each other.

title: In defense of the generalist
excerpt: The advice is always to specialize. I went the other way — AI, full-stack, and security — and the range turned out to be the advantage, not the compromise.

body.text: For a while I worried that working across AI, engineering, and security meant I would be mediocre at all three. The opposite happened. The connections between them became the thing I was actually good at.
Specialists go deep. Generalists go between. Products need both, but the in-between is lonelier and more valuable than people admit.
Range is not a lack of focus. For me it is the focus — on the seams where these fields meet, which is exactly where the interesting problems live.
